text:
Admission Date:  2002-12-21              Discharge Date:   1996-06-12


Service: CARDIOTHORACIC

Allergies:
Patient recorded as having No Known Allergies to Drugs

Attending:Carolyn
Chief Complaint:
changes in bowel habits

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Emergency off-pump CABG x4: LIMA->LAD,SVG->diagonal, ramus and
PDA 05-22


History of Present Illness:
84 yo M presented to OSH 06-27 with chief c/o change in bowel
habits. ROS revealed 2-3 days of chest pain PTA. Cath showed
3VD, transferred to Kern Valley Healthcare District for surgical eval.

Past Medical History:
HTN, ^chol, PVD, type 2 DM, BPH, bladder tumor resection, s/p
partial colectomy for colon CA, s/p cataract surgery, s/p carpal
tunnel release, s/p R sided CVA 1 y ago-no residual

Social History:
retired machanical engineer
lives with wife
remote tobacco
denies etoh

Family History:
Father with MI at age 62

Physical Exam:
Neuro
A&0x3
Strength grip equal bilaterally, plantar flexion/extension equal
bilatterally.
Pupils unequal right 3mm RRLA, Left irregular ~5mm reactive
CV RRR
Lungs CTAB
Abdomen benign
Extrem 1+femoral pulses, 2+radial pulses, DP/PT not palp
bilatterally.
right carotid bruit
warm, no varicosities

TWO-VIEW CHEST, 1982-07-23
Cardiac and mediastinal contours are stable compared to the
prior postoperative radiograph. Lung volumes appear slightly
increased compared to the prior study, and areas of bibasilar
atelectasis show interval improvement, particularly on the left.
Small pleural effusions show minimal improvement.

IMPRESSION: Improving bibasilar atelectasis and slight
improvement in pleural effusions.


Brief Hospital Course:
Mr. Bass was admitted to cardiac surgery for preoperative
workup. That evening he returned from chest xray diaphorectic
and minimally responsive. After return to bed he stated that he
felt better, SBP improved from 80 in wheelchair to 90-100 in
bed. He then complained of chest pain and was taken for emergent
of pump CABG on 05-22. He was transferred to the ICU in critical
but stable condition. He was extubated later that same day. He
was transferred to the floor on POD #1. He did well
postoperatively. His creatinine increased to 1.7 on POD #5, his
medications were adjusted and his creatinine improved. His
creatinine should be rechecked several days after discharge. He
was ready for discharge to rehab on POD #6.

Medications on Admission:
glyburide 5", glucophage 500", atenolol 25', terazosyn 10',
zocor 40', Ec ASA 160', HCTZ 12.5', cozaar 50"

Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO DAILY (Daily).

(Daily): x 3 months.

(Daily).
7. Glyburide 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2 times a

8. Hydrocodone-Acetaminophen 5-500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet
PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed.
9. Lactulose 10 g/15 mL Syrup Sig: Thirty (30) ML PO Q6H (every
6 hours) as needed.
10. Ferrous Gluconate 300 (35) mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO
DAILY (Daily).
11. Ascorbic Acid 500 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO BID (2
times a day).
12. Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg Tablet Sig: 0.5 Tablet PO DAILY
(Daily).
13. Terazosin 10 mg Capsule Sig: One (1) Capsule PO HS (at
bedtime).


Discharge Disposition:

HTN, ^chol, PVD, type 2 DM, BPH, bladder tumor resection, s/p
partial colectomy for colon CA, s/p cataract surgery, s/p carpal

Discharge Condition:

Discharge Instructions:
Call with fever, redness or drainage from incision or weight
gain more than 2 pounds in one day or five in one week.
Shower, no baths, no lotions, creams or powders to incisions.
No lifting more than 10 pounds or driving until follow up with
surgeon.

Followup Instructions:
Dr. Reyes 2 weeks
Dr. Li 2 weeks
Dr. Lauren 4 weeks


                             Kathryn Davis Donald Soto MD 27312
